sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 30-year-old male, presents with a several-week history of right shoulder pain, which began after a period of extensive yard work.
He describes the pain as sore and tender on the outside of the right shoulder, particularly when raising his arm.
He has a history of occasional pain in the same shoulder from playing baseball in his youth, but this current episode is distinct from his previous experiences.
The pain is exacerbated by movement and is not present when he is at rest.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of occasional shoulder pain, which he attributes to his dominant right hand.
Review of systems: The patient denies numbness in his arm or hands.
Current medications: I prescribed ibuprofen (Advil) as needed for pain.
Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's vital signs are: temperature 97.8°F, pulse 62, respirations 16, blood pressure 116/84, and oxygen saturation 99%.
Physical exam: The patient has limited active and passive range of motion in the right shoulder, with tenderness at the greater tuberosity of the humerus.
The acromioclavicular and sternoclavicular joints are not tender.
The patient has a good hand grip, and neurovascular exam of the right arm is intact, with capillary refill less than 3 seconds and sensation to touch intact.
Test Results: The patient's shoulder X-ray is negative for fracture.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient's symptoms are most likely due to rotator cuff tendinopathy, which is an injury to the tendons of the muscles that help make up the shoulder muscles.
Problem list: Rotator cuff tendinopathy; Active; Suspected; Past shoulder injuries; Unknown; Sore feet; Active; Unknown; Dehydration; Suspected.
Plan:
I would like to order an MRI to further evaluate the patient's shoulder and confirm the diagnosis of rotator cuff tendinopathy.
I also want to refer the patient to physical therapy for approximately 6-8 weeks to address the condition.
During this time, I recommend continuing the use of ibuprofen as needed for pain relief.
If the patient's symptoms do not improve, we can consider a steroid injection into the shoulder to provide further relief.
Follow-up: The patient will follow up in 2 weeks to assess the effectiveness of the physical therapy and MRI results.
